topic: The Age of the Crusades c1071-1204
subject: history

items.q: Why did Pope Urban II call the First Crusade in 1095?
items.correct: He responded to a Byzantine appeal for military aid against the Seljuk Turks and framed the campaign as recovering the Holy Land for Christendom.
items.misconception: The Crusade was launched to convert all Muslims to Christianity by force.
items.random_wrong: Urban II called it to conquer France.
items.why: Students assume forced conversion was the goal; the stated aim was reclaiming territory and aiding fellow Christians, with mixed religious and political motives.

items.q: What did the Fourth Crusade actually achieve in 1204?
items.correct: It sacked Constantinople, a Christian city, never reaching the Holy Land, severely damaging Byzantium rather than fighting Muslim powers.
items.misconception: The Fourth Crusade recaptured Jerusalem for the Christians.
items.why: Learners assume every crusade reached the Holy Land; the Fourth was diverted by Venetian and political pressures into attacking fellow Christians.

items.q: Were the Crusader States permanent conquests?
items.correct: They were fragile and gradually lost, with Jerusalem falling to Saladin in 1187, despite repeated later crusades to defend Outremer.
items.misconception: Once captured, Jerusalem stayed in Christian hands for centuries.
items.random_wrong: The Crusader States covered all of the Middle East.
items.why: Students overstate Latin control; Outremer was a narrow, vulnerable strip dependent on Western reinforcement and soon eroded.

items.q: Whose 1095 sermon at Clermont launched the First Crusade?
items.why: Correct answer: Pope Urban II. “Pope Gregory VII” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which 1071 battle did the Seljuks defeat the Byzantines?
items.why: It's Manzikert. “Hattin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which leader recaptured Jerusalem after the Battle of Hattin in 1187?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Saladin. “Richard I” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What unexpected target did the Fourth Crusade sack in 1204?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Constantinople. “Jerusalem” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the First Crusade capture Jerusalem?
items.why: The real figure is 1099 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
